coredata.dc:title: On-site, rapid, and facile determination of gentamicin using a fluorescent resonance energy transfer sensor constructed from nitrogen-carbon quantum dots functionalized by 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shan
Wang, Wenbo
Li, Meng
Zhang, Lirong
Cai, Chenyun
Niu, Baiting
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, NC QDs were obtained from agricultural waste (wheat straw), functionalized by 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid and used to detect gentamicin in aqueous solution. The NC QDs were characterized by HRTEM, XPS, UV–Vis, etc. The fluorescence properties of the functionalized NC QDs for GEN detection were studied at λem = 385 nm. The fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship with the GEN concentration of samples (I = 343.10 + 30.07C, R2 = 0.9896) for GEN concentrations ranging from 0 to 2.9 × 10−4 mol/L. The sensing mechanism was found to be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the functionalized NC QDs and GEN. The results indicated that the method had good repeatability for GEN detection. The technology could be extended to biological detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 45-imidazole dicarboxylic acid
NC QDs
Gentamicin
Biosensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst otherkwds primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon QDs from wheat straw
The characteristics of the functionalized NC QDs
The effects of pH on the fluorescence properties of the C QDs before and after the addition of GEN
The detection of GEN by fluorescence
Analytical application to real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characteristics of QDs (A, TEM micrograph of NC QDs; B, TEM micrograph of functionalized NC QDs; C, FTIR spectra of C QDs; and D, UV–Vis spectra).
XPS spectra of functionalized NC QDs functionalized with imidazole 4,5 dicarboxylic acid (A, functionalized NC QDs; B , C spectra; C, O spectra; and D,N spectra).
The effect of the pH on the fluorescence efficiency was investigated by recording the fluorescence spectrum of functionalized QDs in the absence and presence of gentamicin.

Fluorescence sensing properties of functionalized NC QDs (A, Sensing properties; B, the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the GEN concentration; C, sensing properties after one month; and D, the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the GEN concentration after one month (CGEN 1, 0; 2, 0.29 × 10−4 mol/L; 3,0.58 × 10−4 mol/L; 4, 0.87 × 10−4 mol/L; 5, 1.16 × 10−4 mol/L; 6, 1.45 × 10−4 mol/L;7, 1.74 × 10−4 mol/L; 8, 2.03 × 10−4 mol/L; 9, 2.32 × 10−4 mol/L;10, 2.61 × 10−4 mol/L;11, 2.9 × 10−4 mol/L).
A possible mechanism for gentamicin detection.

Interference study of metal ions and similar derivatives (A 1: GEN, 2: Al3+, 3: Ni2+, 4: Ca2+, 5: Bi3+, 6: Fe3+, 7: Cr3+, 8: Hg2+, 9: Cd2+, 10: Cu2+, 11: Mn2+, 12: Mg2+, 13: Na+, 14: Pb2+, 15 : Sr2+, 16: Zn2+ B 1: GEN, 2: cefotaxime sodium, 3: amikacin sulfate, 4: cefazoxime sodium sulfonamides, 5: cefazolin sodium, 6: cephaloperazone sodium, 7: ceftriaxone sodium).
Recovery of GEN from real samples.

head.ce:title: On-site, rapid, and facile determination of gentamicin using a fluorescent resonance energy transfer sensor constructed from nitrogen-carbon quantum dots functionalized by 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xianyang Normal University, Xianyang 72000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xianyang Normal University, Xianyang 72000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, NC QDs were obtained from agricultural waste (wheat straw), functionalized by 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid and used to detect gentamicin in aqueous solution. The NC QDs were characterized by HRTEM, XPS, UV–Vis, etc. The fluorescence properties of the functionalized NC QDs for GEN detection were studied at λem = 385 nm. The fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship with the GEN concentration of samples (I = 343.10 + 30.07C, R2 = 0.9896) for GEN concentrations ranging from 0 to 2.9 × 10−4 mol/L. The sensing mechanism was found to be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the functionalized NC QDs and GEN. The results indicated that the method had good repeatability for GEN detection. The technology could be extended to biological detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Improper use of antibiotics leads to drug resistance and in vivo flora disorders and poses other hazards. For instance, penicillin use can cause shock or even death. Aminoglycoside antibiotics were so named because their molecular structures are formed by connecting one aminocyclol to one or more amino sugar quanta by sugar bonds. Most aminoglycoside antibiotics are generally injected because of being soluble and difficult to absorb by the gastrointestinal tract. Aminoglycoside antibiotics bind to bacterial ribosomes and interfere with bacterial protein synthesis (Farouk, Azzazy, & Niessen, 2015; Schoukroun-Barnes, Wagan, & White, 2014). Therefore, these antibiotics have wide applications in the medical field. GEN can combine with ribosomes in 30 s and effectively prevent bacterial protein synthesis. GEN has strong inhibitory effects on both gram-negative and gram-positive bacterial infections in livestock and humans and is widely used in disease treatment and prevention (Chen, Shang, Li, Wu, & Xiao, 2008; Li et al., 2017]. However, excessive levels of GEN in animal-derived foods can have adverse effects on human health. For example, excessive GEN doses have been found to increase the incidence of ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity (He et al., 2016; Li et al., 2016). The primary methods for detecting GEN are microbiological assays (Moayad et al., 2017), LC-MS (Stephanie et al., 2017), HPLC (Zhang et al., 2015), ELISA (Wang, Yang, He, Huang, & Kang, 2005), and immunoassays (Wei, Wang, Juan, & Lai, 2009; Peng et al., 2009). However, using HPLC and LC-MS to detect gentamicin has disadvantages, such as the requirement of very expensive instrumentation, as well as specialized and highly trained operators, whereas microbiological assays have poor sensitivity and are time-consuming to carry out. Fluorescence analysis has attracted the attention of analytical chemists for high sensitivity and adaptability to high-throughput formats.

Fluorescence analysis has been extensively utilized in medicine, environmental analysis, food analysis, and other fields for simplicity, reproducibility, and high sensitivity (Wang, Xu, Zhao, & Hong, 2014; Liu, Xue, Wang, Yang, & Lv, 2012; Beloglazova, Shmelin, & Eremin, 2016). The reaction of the amino group of GEN with the carboxyl group of nitrogen-carbon quantum dots (NC QDs) functionalized by 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid (hereafter referred to as functionalized NC QDs) results in strong fluorescence. Therefore, fluorescence analysis can be used to detect GEN. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of NC QDs was found to be linearly related to the GEN concentration over a range of 0–2.9 × 10−4mol/L. High selectivity of fluorescence for GEN detection was observed. The results indicated that functionalized NC QDs are a promising candidate for GEN detection as novel and pollution-free QDs derived from agricultural waste. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first attempt at using the FRET mechanism of NC QDs in aqueous solution to detect GEN on-site, rapidly, and easily. Furthermore, the developed sensor has high sensitivity, good selectivity and requires relatively few samples and can therefore be used to detect actual complex samples. The application of the sensor to the detection of antibiotics offers advantages such as online detection, cost-effectiveness, and point-of-care application.

Gentamicin (GEN) was purchased from Changzhi Sanbao Biochemistry Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. China (Changzhi, Shanxi, China). 4,5-Imidazole dicarboxylic acid was purchased from Xintai Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Xianyang, Shaanxi, China). All the reagents were analytical grade. Ultrapure water was prepared from secondary distilled water.

The UV–Vis spectra of the NC QDs and functionalized NC QDs were obtained using a UV5800 spectrometer. The fluorescence properties of the samples were detected using an F-7000 spectrofluorometer, and the emission and excitation spectra were measured using a 10-nm slit width. The absorbance was measured via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy in KBr mode by using a Thermo Nicolet Avatar 370. The morphology of the NC QDs and the functionalized NC QDs was examined via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL/JEM 2100, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon QDs from wheat straw
ce:section.ce:para: First, wheat straw was washed, crushed into a powder, and dried at 80 °C in an oven for 6 h. Afterwards, 35 mL of a 3 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution was added to 0.5 g of the dried wheat straw powder. The solution was stirred for 10 mins and poured into a 50-mL Teflon-lined high-temperature reactor. The reactor was placed in a drying box, and the temperature was adjusted to 250 °C for 10 h (Wang, Fu, Zhang, Huan, Liu, & Zeng, 2020). The reactor was cooled to 20 °C, after which the sample was retrieved. C QDs were obtained.

First, 0.16 g of 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine amine was added to 30 mL of the unmodified C QD solution. The solution was then magnetically stirred at room temperature for 10 mins. The solution was placed in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and reacted in an oven for 1 h at 180 °C. NC QDs were obtained by hydrothermal reaction.

Subsequently, 0.03 g of 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid was added to 10 mL of the NC QDs. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 1 h to obtain 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid functionalized NC QDs (hereafter referred to as functionalized NC QDs) as a sensor. The obtained solution was stored at 4 °C.

The functionalized NC QDs were used to detect GEN over a concentration range of 0 to –2.9 × 10−4 mol/L in this study. First, 100 μL of the functionalized NC QDs were added to 3 mL of each of the 0–2.9 × 10−4 mol/L GEN solutions. The fluorescence intensity of the functionalized NC QDs in the absence of GEN was denoted by F0 , whereas the fluorescence intensity at different GEN concentrations was denoted by F. The fluorescence intensity was measured at λex = 340 nm and λem = 385 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characteristics of the functionalized NC QDs
ce:section.ce:para: A TEM image of the QDs is presented in Fig. 1 A. Uniformly sized NC QDs (2–3 nm) were obtained after hydrothermal synthesis at 180 °C for approximately 12 h. A conventional HRTEM image of the QDs is presented in Fig. 1B and clearly shows the atomic arrangement of the QDs. Compressive residual strain probably existed in the QD sample because of the lattice mismatch among the elemental components. The 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid was mainly modified at the interface of the NC QDs and could be distinguished from the NC QDs (Xu et al., 2015). The sizes of the NC QDs after functionalization by 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid were approximately 4–5 nm, and the largest NC QDs were approximately 6–7 nm in size, which probably resulted from the interaction of the surface groups.

The FTIR spectra of the C QDs are shown in Fig. 1C. The spectral peaks at 3296, 1742 and 1597 cm−1 could be ascribed to OH stretching and carboxyl/carbonyl CO and CC vibrations, respectively (Chen et al., 2016). The spectral peaks of the C QDs at 3350, 1640 and 1023 cm−1 could be attributed to OH, CO, and CC, respectively (Tang, Zhu, He, & Yang, 2017; Xue, Zhan, Zou, Zhang, & Zhao, 2016). The FTIR and XPS analyses confirmed these observations. Thus, C QDs have potential biochemical applications, such as drug delivery and detection (Li et al., 2014; Mewada, Pandey, Thakur, Jadhav, & Sharon, 2014).

The UV–Vis spectra of the C QDs, NC QDs and functionalized NC QDs exhibited an absorption shoulder at 340 nm. As shown in Fig. 1D, the absorption spectra were characteristic of NC QDs and functionalized NC QDs in the quantum size regime because the absorption peaks of these QDs did not shift compared with those of the C QDs. Moreover, the sharpness of the absorption shoulders indicated that the functionalized NC QDs had a narrow size distribution. The results of a UV–Vis study demonstrated that the NC QD particles were in the quantum size regime. The particles were estimated to be 2–3 nm in size using the effective mass approximation equation. An image of the aqueous N-doped C QDs in a 3-mL cuvette under a white-light-and-UV lamp is presented in the inset of Fig. 1D. Excitation-dependent fluorescence was observed, which is common in fluorescent carbon materials (Shi et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2015; Sharma et al., 2016). This behaviour contributed to the surface state of the N-doped C QDs. The surface state was analogous to a molecular state, wherein the size effect was a result of the quantum dimensions, which contributed to the complexity of the excited states of the N-doped C QDs (Gude, Das, Chatterjee, & Mandal, 2016).

The XPS spectra of the functionalized NC QDs are shown in Fig. 2 . These spectra indicated the presence of N, O, and C in the QD solution and confirmed the presence of these elements on the surface of the functionalized NC QDs (Fig. 2A). A C1s peak appeared at 284 eV in the background (Fig. 2B). The fluorescence mechanism in this case was the surface state transition of CO functional groups (n-π*) attached to the C QDs, and almost all the excited electrons returned to the ground state via radiative energy transfer (Liu et al., 2018; Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2015; Li & Dong, 2018; Mintz, Zhou, & Leblanc, 2019).

The asymmetric profile of O1s exhibited two symmetrical peaks at 530.43 and 531.69 eV (Fig. 2C), indicating that the functionalized QDs contained two different oxide-related species, which could be attributed to CO and COC/C–OH. The N1s spectrum exhibited peaks at 396 eV, 398 eV (pyridinic N) and 401.0 eV (pyrrolic N) (Fig. 2D) (Vedamalai et al., 2014; Qian et al., 2014). The peaks at 402 and 404 eV were possibly due to the formation of NH3 + in the QDs (Gao, Traeger, Wöll, & Idriss, 2014). The XPS analysis results revealed that the surface of the C QDs was functionalized by 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of pH on the fluorescence properties of the C QDs before and after the addition of GEN
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different pH values on the fluorescence intensity of the C QDs are illustrated in Fig. 3 A and 3B. The fluorescence of the QDs gradually increased over pHs 1–7 and gradually decreased over pHs 7–14. This trend may have been observed because the NC QDs effectively dissociated owing to the maximum increase in H+ ions at pH 7. As the OH− concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity decreased as the pH increased from 7 to 14.

The effects of the different pHs on the fluorescence intensity of the C QDs in the presence of GEN were analysed. The results are illustrated in Fig. 3C and 3D. The fluorescence intensity decreased after GEN interacted with the NC QDs under acidic conditions. As the pH decreased, the reaction between the NC QDs and GEN produced a precipitate. There are five amino groups on the GEN surface, and the degree of protonation increased with the acidity. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased as the pH increased from 1 to 7. Additionally, NC QD carriers can be completely biodegraded in acidic environments. The fluorescence intensity substantially decreased over pH 7–14 because the carboxyl groups of the NC QDs reacted with the five basic amino groups of GEN (alkaline centres, the pKa in water was approximately 7.5–7.9). Hence, the fluorescence intensity decreased as the pH increased over the 8–14 range. The highest fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 7 when GEN was added to the NC QD system. The fluorescence intensity was evidently higher under alkaline conditions than under acidic conditions. However, competitive combination between the QDs and GEN caused the fluorescence intensity to decrease (Qian et al., 2014). Accordingly, pH 7 was chosen as the optimal pH for GEN detection in the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of GEN by fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: At pH 7, the fluorescence intensity of the functionalized NC QDs rapidly increased with the addition of 10 μM GEN to the solution. The fluorescence intensity reached a minimum within 2 mins (the optimal time) and remained constant thereafter. The effects of the GEN concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the functionalized NC QDs were investigated under prescribed conditions.

The fluorescence intensity of the NC QDs gradually increased as the GEN concentration increased from 0 to 2.9 × 10−4 mol/L, and the corresponding linear equation I = 343.10 + 30.07C had a correlation coefficient of 0.9892 (Fig. 4 A and 4B).

The fluorescence spectra and the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the NC QDs and the GEN concentration one month after preparation of the NC QDs are shown in Fig. 4C and 4D, respectively. The fluorescence properties of the NC QDs remained stable and linear with a corresponding correlation coefficient of 0.9956. The results showed the clear response of GEN to the NC QDs. Therefore, the NC QDs can detect GEN.

The NC QDs functionalized by 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid could serve as an energy donor. The oxygen atoms in the carboxyl group (–COOH) of the C QDs interacted with the amine group (–NH2) in GEN to form intermolecular hydrogen bonds. When the QDs were excited, FRET occurred between the NC QDs and GEN (with GEN acting as an energy receptor). The mechanism is depicted in Fig. 5 .

Industrial and human activities can release common metal ions and antibiotics into water. The selectivity of the proposed detection sensor was determined by performing experiments on the structural analogues of GEN and metal ions. The presence of foreign ions changed the fluorescence intensity by approximately ± 5% (Fig. 6 A); therefore, metal ions interfered minimally with GEN detection.

The NC QDs were allowed to interact with different antibiotics at the same concentration, and the antibiotics were found to have different effects on the fluorescence intensity of the NC QDs (Fig. 6B). The highest fluorescence intensity was observed upon adding cefoperazone sodium and sulbactam sodium to a NC QD solution (Fig. 6B), and the next highest fluorescence intensity was recorded upon addition of cefazolin sodium, cefotaxime sodium, ceftriaxone sodium, amikacin sulfate, and cefazoxime sodium sulfonamides to an NC QD solution. This result may have been obtained because cefoperazone sodium and sulbactam sodium interfered with the electrostatic interaction between amino and carboxyl groups. In a previous study, QDs were also used to determine the content of pajusa star but failed to determine the content of other compounds containing NH2 (Ling, 2008).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application to real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The same GEN concentrations (in the 0 to 2.9 × 10−4 mol/L range) were used in different samples, namely, double-distilled water (DDW), Xianyang Lake water, Qinchi water and park water. The functionalized NC QDs were added to the samples at the same concentration. The mixtures were incubated for 2 mins at room temperature, and the fluorescence intensity was measured at λex = 340 nm and λem = 385 nm.

Solutions were obtained by adding a known quantity of GEN to the samples. The resulting samples were tested to determine whether the added GEN quantity could be recovered. The GEN recovery rate ranged from 100% to 109% (Table 1 ). However, the efficiency of the functionalized NC QDs in detecting GEN in different samples was different. Nevertheless, this difference did not affect the detection rate for actual samples.

Water-dispersible and luminescent NC QDs were obtained from wheat straw and functionalized by 4,5-imidazole dicarboxylic acid. The blue fluorescent NC QDs were approximately 5 nm in size. These NC QDs were characterized via FTIR spectroscopy, XPS, TEM analysis, fluorescence analysis, and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The fluorescence properties of the functionalized NC QDs were used to detect various GEN concentrations via a quenching mechanism mediated by FRET between the NC QDs and GEN. A linear response was obtained for GEN concentrations ranging from 0 to 2.9 × 10−4 mol/L. In summary, the NC QDs were found to be a nontoxic, rapid, selective, and simple detection agent for GEN in aqueous systems. The novel sensor could successfully detect GEN in real water samples with satisfactory accuracy and recovery. This detection method is simple and fast and has good application potential for detecting GEN residues in aqueous solution.

Shan Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Wenbo Wang: Investigation. Meng Li: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology. Lirong Zhang: Resources. Chenyun Cai: Investigation. Baiting Niu: Revise.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21703189); Xianyang Science and Technology Bureau Project (2019K02–27); National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872175); Key Research and Development Program of Shaanxi Province (2019NY-103); and Xianyang Normal University Project (2019K0237).
